title: Centrum & The Canal Ring
standfirst: The historic UNESCO-listed core of Amsterdam, where 17th-century wealth meets 21st-century tourism. Its concentric rings of water define the city's character.
intro_html:
Amsterdam's city centre is a masterpiece of 17th-century urban planning. The 'Grachtengordel' (Canal Ring) was built as part of a massive expansion project during the Dutch Golden Age, creating four main concentric canals — Singel, Herengracht, Keizersgracht, and Prinsengracht — that still serve as the city's blueprint.

While it is the most tourist-heavy part of Amsterdam, the Centrum remains extraordinary for its architecture alone. Every street corner offers a view of tilting gabled houses and stone bridges that have stood for four hundred years. This is where you'll find the Royal Palace, the Oude Kerk, and the busiest shopping streets in the Netherlands.

todos.title: The Royal Palace (Paleis op de Dam)
todos.description:
Standing proudly on Dam Square, the Royal Palace is one of the most significant monuments of the Dutch Golden Age. Originally built as the city hall in the 17th century, it was intended to be the '8th wonder of the world,' a testament to the city's global seafaring power and wealth.

Inside, the massive Citizen's Hall features marble maps embedded in the floor, showing the world as it was known when Amsterdam was at its peak. The palace is still used by the Dutch Royal House for state visits and gala dinners, but it is open to the public for tours that offer a rare look at royal living and monumental 17th-century architecture.

todos.title: Begijnhof
todos.description:
The Begijnhof is a medieval sanctuary tucked away behind a modest wooden door on the Spui. Once an 'almshouse' for professional religious women (Beguines), it is now a peaceful, residential courtyard that feels a world away from the surrounding high-street shopping of the Kalverstraat.

It features one of the two remaining wooden houses in Amsterdam, dating back to 1420. Walking through the quiet lawns, surrounded by private houses and a clandestine Catholic chapel, offers a rare glimpse into the medieval and early modern life of the city. It remains a place of residence, so visitors are asked to maintain a respectful silence.

todos.title: De Bijenkorf
todos.description:
De Bijenkorf ('The Beehive') is Amsterdam's premier luxury department store, a grand institution that has defined upscale Dutch shopping for over 150 years. The flagship building on Dam Square is a masterpiece of early 20th-century design, featuring a soaring atrium and cutting-edge window displays.

Beyond the world-class designer fashion and accessories, the store's top floor houses an incredible glass-roofed restaurant area that offers panoramic views of the city center. It is the perfect place for a sophisticated break during a day of sightseeing, offering everything from artisan patisserie to high-end international cuisine.

todos.title: Canal Cruise
todos.description:
There is no better way to understand the architecture and history of Amsterdam than from the water. A standard one-hour cruise takes you through the UNESCO-protected canal ring, under historic stone bridges, and past the tilting merchant houses of the Golden Age.

Many operators depart from directly in front of Centraal Station, but 'open-boat' tours led by local captains provide a more intimate and customizable experience. Whether you're here for the history or just the views, seeing the city from its primary waterways is an essential Amsterdam rite of passage that provides a perspective you simply can't get from the street.

foods.title: Pancake Bakery
foods.description:
Located in a massive 17th-century warehouse along the Prinsengracht, the Pancake Bakery is an Amsterdam institution. They specialize in traditional Dutch pancakes (pannenkoeken) that are as large as a pizza and thin like a crepe, topped with an incredible array of savory or sweet ingredients.

The original brick walls and massive wooden beams of the warehouse have been preserved, creating an incredibly atmospheric and rustic dining environment. It is a favorite for both locals and travelers, especially families, offering a hearty and authentic taste of Dutch comfort food in one of the city's most beautiful historic settings.

foods.title: Café Hoppe
foods.description:
Dating back to 1670, Café Hoppe is one of the most famous 'brown cafes' in the Netherlands. It was originally a haunt for the city's literary and political elite, and it still retains its authentic 17th-century atmosphere with sand on the floor and wall-to-wall oak barrels.

The café is split into a historic standing-room side and a more modern (though still very traditional) lounge side. It is the perfect place to sample a mature Dutch cheese plate and a glass of local jenever while soaking in the history of the city core. It's a place where time seems to have stood still for three centuries.

products.title: Classic Canal Cruise
products.description: A 75-minute cruise through the UNESCO canal ring with live commentary from a local captain.

getting_there.text: The entire area is walkable from Centraal Station.
Trams 2, 4, 12, 13, 14, 17 all serve the direct center.
